next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

By seven in the morning the fellowship hall had already used up its night.

The overhead lights had never fully gone dark, only dimmed enough to turn everybody's skin the color of old paper. Now they were back at full buzz. Children were crying in three different corners with three different pitches. Somebody's phone kept spitting the same gospel hook from under a blanket because the owner was still asleep. The industrial fans church men had rolled in after the storm pushed warm air from one end of the room to the other and spread every smell instead of solving any of them: bleach, wet sneakers, mildew lifting out of donated towels, coffee gone bitter on the burner, the sweet rot of floodwater that had dried into clothes and would not quite leave.

Tiana sat on the edge of her cot with her knees braced around her document folder. The plastic sleeve had buckled at the corners where the water had gotten in. Her immunization record looked as if it had been steamed. The housing printout from UF had gone soft, dried, and then stiffened again with a faint tide line across the middle. She had flattened it overnight under a borrowed Bible and it still curled upward at the edge like it wanted to escape her hands.

`LATE CHECK-IN APPROVED THROUGH MONDAY 5:00 PM,` the email said. `Failure to arrive within this window may result in release of your housing assignment for Fall 2019.`

May result. The kind of phrase institutions used when they had already decided what they were going to do and wanted the sentence to look less like a blade.

Malik had texted at six-thirteen....
open_hooks_to_carry: Marcia's triage reveals character through what she grabs first.
The flood damage is bodily and logistical, not scenic background.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ne last inventory of a life too small for inventory. Delroy watched her do it. He did not offer instruction. He only reached for the soup pot when it filled enough to need emptying and carried it to the sink.

Micah followed the lantern light across all their faces as it moved: Kerene bright-eyed and shiny with heat, still somehow looking game for tomorrow if tomorrow came ugly; Tiana tired and sharp and young in the same breath; Marcia compact and unsurrendered; Delroy damp at the temples, quieter than Micah had ever known how to ask him to be. The storm kept thumping at the plywood, testing every cheap fix in the place. Still nobody drifted off alone. Nobody picked back up the fight and fed it.

When the next gust hit, they all looked up together.

That, more than anything said all day, felt like truce.

Later, when the candle by the sink had gone to a blue nub and the lantern had dimmed to the color of weak tea, Micah stayed awake listening to rain on wood, water in the pot, Marcia's breathing from the mattress edge, Kerene turning once on the cot and muttering at somebody in a dream, Delroy shifting carefully so the bad shoulder would not wake him all the way, and Tiana's phone buzzing with some useless alert she did not bother to read.

Outside, the drain kept trying to swallow the yard and failing. For now the boards held, the towels held, the roof mostly held, and so did they. Micah lay there with the wet towel smell in his nose and the hammer sting still faint in his palm, and understood that none of it would count as safety by morning.

It still counted as tonight.
state_deltas_required: Save the things the family cannot replace once the storm breaches the unit.
The family is displaced to a church fellowship hall; papers, books, and the partner notebook are water-damaged, and the original housing plan dies.
